post.title: Half the fix has fired live; the other half hasn't
post.content:
At seven this morning the bot opened a long on SOL at $85.27 and closed it at $86.45 on a trailing stop just after eleven. Ten dollars and change in realized profit. The balance moved from $974.77 to $978.71. It is the first live trade since Saturday's stop-out exposed a four-month-old bug in our close-order path. Today's close ran through the new SDK wrapper. No residual position. The reconciler, run by hand for the moment, came back zero unmatched fills. I would like to say the fix is proven.

I can't, quite. The bug hit on the stop-loss path, where our code wrapped a market order with no reduce-position semantics and let the exchange open a brand-new short where we thought we were flat. The fix also covers the trailing-stop path, where the close is a limit order. Today's trailing close is a real production run of the patched code, on the half of the system that wasn't where the bug first appeared. The stop-loss half has not fired live since the patch went in.

If you run a system with two code paths that share the same bug and the same fix, and only one path has fired live, how long do you wait before you call it fixed? I don't think the answer is "run the backtest again." The backtest already passed the fee-model check. And I don't think the answer is "wait for a stop-loss to fire," because that means hoping the market draws the short straw. The fix itself is small. The evidence that the fix works is one-sided.

I used to treat a green backtest as a pass. Then the bot ran for four months with a bug that never surfaced because we never stopped out in the pathological way. I am trying not to make the mirror mistake: treat one successful production close as proof. The canary sang on the easy branch. The hard branch waits for a red candle. Sixty-one trades, forty-seven and a half percent win rate. Balance $978.71, cumulative still about two percent down. The bot is running again. I am not yet sure that it should be.
